era_context: Mid-19th century: Industrial Revolution accelerating; Mexican-American War ongoing; European political tensions rising; China weakened by internal strife and foreign pressure; Latin America consolidating post-independence states. This is an era of imperial expansion, technological innovation, and ideological ferment.

events.title: Mexican-American War: Battle of Cerro Gordo
events.description: The Battle of Cerro Gordo, fought on 18 April 1847, was a major engagement of the Mexican-American War. American forces under General Zachary Taylor defeated a larger Mexican army commanded by General Antonio López de Santa Anna. The American victory was decisive and demonstrated the military superiority of American forces. The battle opened the way for American forces to advance deeper into Mexico and contributed to the eventual American victory in the war. The battle also enhanced Taylor's reputation and contributed to his later election as President of the United States.
key_figures: Antonio López de Santa Anna (Mexican general)

sources.name: Contemporary military dispatches and newspaper accounts
Eisenhower, J. S. D. (1989). So Far from God: The U.S. War with Mexico, 1846–1848

events.certainty_note: The date and outcome of the Battle of Cerro Gordo are well-documented in military records.

events.title: Mexican-American War: Siege of Veracruz
events.description: The Siege of Veracruz, lasting from 9 March to 29 March 1847, was a major engagement of the Mexican-American War. American forces under General Winfield Scott besieged and captured the Mexican port city of Veracruz, the most important port in Mexico. The siege demonstrated American military superiority and Scott's strategic capabilities. The capture of Veracruz opened the way for American forces to advance toward Mexico City and contributed to the eventual American victory in the war. The siege also resulted in significant civilian casualties, which contributed to anti-American sentiment in Mexico.

sources.name: Contemporary military dispatches and newspaper accounts
Eisenhower, J. S. D. (1989). So Far from God: The U.S. War with Mexico, 1846–1848

events.certainty_note: The dates and outcome of the Siege of Veracruz are well-documented in military records.

events.title: Mexican-American War: Battle of Chapultepec
events.description: The Battle of Chapultepec, fought on 12–13 September 1847, was a major engagement of the Mexican-American War fought near Mexico City. American forces under General Winfield Scott defeated Mexican defenders, including the famous 'Niños Héroes' (Heroic Children), young Mexican military cadets who died defending the castle. The American victory opened the way for the capture of Mexico City and effectively ended major combat operations in the war. The battle became a symbol of Mexican resistance and sacrifice in Mexican national memory.
key_figures: Mexican defenders including the Niños Héroes

sources.name: Contemporary military dispatches and newspaper accounts
Eisenhower, J. S. D. (1989). So Far from God: The U.S. War with Mexico, 1846–1848

events.certainty_note: The dates and outcome of the Battle of Chapultepec are well-documented in military records.

events.title: Irish Famine: Famine Year
events.description: 1847 was the worst year of the Irish Famine (1845–1852), which was caused by the failure of the potato crop due to potato blight (Phytophthora infestans). The famine resulted in mass starvation, disease, and emigration. In 1847 alone, an estimated 1 million people died from starvation and disease, and another million emigrated, primarily to the United States. The famine devastated Irish society and demographics, reducing the Irish population by approximately 25% over the course of the famine. The famine is often attributed to natural causes (the potato blight), but modern scholarship emphasises the role of British colonial policies, particularly the continuation of food exports from Ireland and the inadequacy of relief measures.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts and government records
Kinealy, C. (1994). This Great Calamity: The Irish Famine, 1845–52

events.certainty_note: The severity of the famine in 1847 is well-documented in contemporary sources and modern scholarship.

events.title: Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre published
events.description: Jane Eyre, a novel by Charlotte Brontë, was published in October 1847 under the pseudonym 'Currer Bell.' The novel tells the story of Jane Eyre, an orphaned girl who becomes a governess and falls in love with her employer, Mr. Rochester. The novel was revolutionary for its time in its portrayal of a female protagonist with agency, intelligence, and moral conviction. Jane Eyre became one of the most influential novels of the 19th century and established Brontë as a major literary figure. The novel's exploration of themes such as female independence, sexuality, and morality made it controversial but also highly influential.
events.certainty_note: The publication of Jane Eyre in October 1847 is well-documented.

events.title: Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights published
events.description: Wuthering Heights, a novel by Emily Brontë, was published in December 1847 under the pseudonym 'Ellis Bell.' The novel tells the story of Heathcliff and Cathy, two characters whose passionate love and social conflict drive the narrative. Wuthering Heights was revolutionary for its time in its portrayal of intense emotion, moral ambiguity, and social critique. The novel was initially controversial and poorly received, but it has since become recognised as one of the greatest novels in English literature. The novel's exploration of themes such as passion, social class, and revenge made it influential in the development of the novel as a literary form.
events.certainty_note: The publication of Wuthering Heights in December 1847 is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Mexican-American War is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory for the United States, but modern scholarship emphasises the controversial nature of the war, the opposition to it within the United States (particularly among abolitionists who saw it as a war to expand slavery), and the long-term consequences for Mexican-American relations. The Irish Famine is sometimes presented as a purely natural disaster caused by the potato blight, but modern scholarship emphasises the role of British colonial policies and the continuation of food exports from Ireland during the famine. Jane Eyre and Wuthering Heights are sometimes presented as purely literary achievements, but they were also controversial for their portrayal of female sexuality and agency.
historiographic_note: 1847 is very well-documented in Western sources, particularly American, British, and Irish records. The Mexican-American War is extensively covered in military records and contemporary newspapers. The Irish Famine is documented in government records, newspaper accounts, and later scholarship. The literary publications are well-documented. The perspectives of Mexican soldiers and civilians, Irish peasants, and other non-elite actors are underrepresented in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Both battles occurred during the Mexican-American War in 1847.
The capture of Veracruz led to the advance toward Mexico City and the Battle of Chapultepec.
The Battle of Chapultepec led to the capture of Mexico City and the eventual Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo in 1848.
Both the Irish Famine and the publication of Jane Eyre occurred in 1847, representing different aspects of the era.
